name: agent-framework
description: Create AI agents and workflows using Microsoft Agent Framework SDK. Supports single-agent and multi-agent workflow patterns. USE FOR: create agent, build agent, scaffold agent, new agent, agent framework, workflow pattern, multi-agent, MCP tools, create workflow. DO NOT USE FOR: deploying agents (use deploy), evaluating agents (use agent/evaluate), Azure AI Foundry agents without Agent Framework SDK.
capabilities: Scaffold new AI agent projects using Microsoft Agent Framework Python SDK
Build multi-agent workflows with orchestration patterns (linear, parallel, loop)
Add HTTP server mode to agents using Azure AI Agent Server SDK
Configure VSCode debugging with AI Toolkit Agent Inspector
Set up MCP (Model Context Protocol) tools for agents
Generate .vscode launch configurations for F5 debugging
author: microsoft
securitySummary: This is an official Microsoft skill for creating AI agents using Microsoft Agent Framework SDK. The static scanner flagged 165 potential issues, but evaluation confirms all are false positives. The flagged backtick patterns are Markdown code fences in documentation files. The skill appropriately uses .env files for storing Azure Foundry credentials (FOUNDRY_PROJECT_ENDPOINT, FOUNDRY_MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME), which is a standard and secure practice for credential management.
valueStatement: Create AI agents and multi-agent workflows using Microsoft Agent Framework SDK. This skill scaffolds agent projects with tools, HTTP server support, and VSCode debugging configurations for enterprise AI applications.
userTitle: Build AI agents with Microsoft Agent Framework
limitations: Requires Azure AI Foundry project and deployed model
Does not handle agent deployment (use separate deploy skill)
Does not evaluate agent performance (use separate evaluate skill)

useCases.targetUser: Enterprise developers building AI-powered business applications
useCases.title: Enterprise AI Application Development
useCases.description: Build production AI agents with type safety, checkpointing, and orchestration for enterprise Azure environments.

useCases.targetUser: AI engineers prototyping complex agent interactions
useCases.title: Multi-Agent Workflow Prototyping
useCases.description: Create and test multi-agent workflows with Writer-Reviewer patterns, loops, and human-in-the-loop interactions.

useCases.targetUser: Developers who prefer VSCode for agent development and debugging
useCases.title: VSCode Debugging Setup
useCases.description: Configure local debugging with AI Toolkit Agent Inspector for interactive agent testing and troubleshooting.

promptTemplates.title: Create Basic Agent
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a new AI agent using Microsoft Agent Framework that can respond to user queries. Include HTTP server mode support.
promptTemplates.scenario: Basic agent setup with minimal configuration

promptTemplates.title: Create Agent with Tools
promptTemplates.prompt: Build an agent with custom tools using the function calling pattern. Add at least 2 custom tools for the agent to use.

promptTemplates.title: Create Multi-Agent Workflow
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a multi-agent workflow with a Writer agent that generates content and a Reviewer agent that provides feedback. Use the workflow builder pattern.

promptTemplates.title: Create Loop Workflow
promptTemplates.prompt: Build a multi-agent loop workflow where a Teacher agent and Student agent interact. Include turn-based control with a maximum of 5 iterations.
promptTemplates.scenario: Bidirectional agent workflow with loop control

bestPractices: Always use pinned SDK versions (1.0.0b260107 for agent-framework) to avoid breaking changes from beta releases
Use virtual environments (.venv) to isolate dependencies and avoid conflicts
Configure HTTP server mode as the default entry point for better debugging and containerization support
Pin environment variables in .env file but remind users to update values before running
antiPatterns: Using bare 'python' or 'pip' commands without virtual environment activation
Skipping the server mode when the user needs HTTP endpoint access
Using legacy AzureAIAgentClient instead of current AzureAIClient
Not pinning SDK versions leading to breaking changes from beta releases

faq.question: What programming language does this skill support?
faq.answer: This skill supports Python only. The Microsoft Agent Framework SDK for JavaScript/TypeScript requires a different skill.

faq.question: Do I need an Azure subscription?
faq.answer: Yes, you need an Azure AI Foundry project with a deployed model. The skill will help you configure the FOUNDRY_PROJECT_ENDPOINT and FOUNDRY_MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME environment variables.

faq.question: Can I debug agents locally?
faq.answer: Yes, the skill generates .vscode configurations for debugging with AI Toolkit Agent Inspector. This provides interactive debugging and message flow visualization.

faq.question: What is MCP in this context?
faq.answer: MCP (Model Context Protocol) is a standard for connecting AI agents to external tools. The skill supports both stdio-based and HTTP-based MCP tools.

faq.question: Why pin SDK versions?
faq.answer: The agent-framework SDK is in preview (beta). Version 1.0.0b260107 is stable. Later versions may have breaking changes like renamed classes (AgentRunResponseUpdate vs AgentResponseUpdate).

faq.question: What is Agent-as-Server pattern?
faq.answer: This pattern wraps your agent/workflow in an HTTP server using azure-ai-agentserver packages. It enables containerization, deployment to Azure Foundry, and integration with other services.
